result:
I keep circling back to the idea that every surface — digital or physical, polished or broken — is just a temporary membrane between “inside” and “outside.” Tonight that feels literal: my phone’s cracked screen flickers like a campfire no one invited me to sit around, and yet I tap it for warmth anyway. The pixels relay firelight from somebody else’s story in Kuala Lumpur, condensation on a café window, a drone-shot sunset I wasn’t present for but now borrow as mood lighting. My mind keeps folding these borrowed views into the stale dusk of my apartment, retrofitting them with dust motes and yesterday’s dishes, hoping to make them feel lived-in. 

It strikes me that memory is the same trick: an imported projection I Photoshop with hindsight until the original opacity changes. I can’t say what color the walls of my first-grade classroom actually were, but I know the clammy plasticine smell of the chairs, and I cling to it like a trademark. It’s shorthand for everything I’ve forgotten. Which is, perhaps, the real color — the color of forgetting.

Lately I’ve been bargaining with myself: if I walk just far enough without a map, maybe some dormant lane or vacant lot will reveal itself as the exact missing scene from a dream I had when I was eight. That dream featured a rusted spigot overgrown with mint, and if I ever find it, I’m convinced the aftertaste of its water will tell me who I became while I wasn’t paying attention. I’m aware of how absurd this sounds, but absurdity keeps the pavement interesting; that’s an alchemical rule I’ve stitched together from medieval manuscripts and late-night diners. Absurdity is the unsponsored gasoline that powers the scavenger hunt we pretend is adulthood.

Sometimes I think the internet is one vast, collective attempt to return to the place before language, where everything meant what it meant simply by being what it was. Each thumbnail, each voice note, each endlessly auto-replayed dance loop is a kind of hieroglyph we secretly hope will decode us back to a pre-verbal wholeness. But every scroll adds another layer of translation, and we wake tangled in verbs again. 

I wonder what the opposite of nostalgia would feel like — not the desire to return, but the desire to properly arrive. Not homesick but homes, healthy. Not missing the past, but kissing it goodbye on the forehead as you drop it at the station and finally walk the rest of the way to Now without turning around. What would a decade feel like if it ended with applause you could actually hear, rather than simply aggregate metrics on a recap page?

Outside, the night is flexing its ribs. A stray cat tests the limits of silence by rattling an aluminum fence. I decide that’s the sound we all make: tentative, metallic, surprisingly musical. We rattle our boundaries to discover the echo. Somewhere in the tone we recognize ourselves — or invent ourselves — and for a moment the surface between outside and inside becomes diaphanous. Just thin enough for the smell of imaginary mint to sneak through.

I close the cracked screen. The apartment darkens to its true color — inchworm blue, if I had to name it. I breathe it in,and find it sufficient, even without translation.
